metadata.title: The interface between research funding and environmental policies in an emergent economy using neural topic modeling: Proposals for a research agenda

abstract:
Researchers have been calling for further analysis of the relationships between academia and policy making. Since several studies are conducted with resources from public funding agencies, it is essential to assess whether governments have been supporting research that can contribute to future environmental policies and whether research has been supporting the elaboration of public policies. To fill these gaps, the objective of this study was to evaluate the similarities and gaps between research and environmental policies. A bibliometric analysis of studies funded by Chilean agencies and text analytics techniques were employed to analyze research and environmental policies. We used BERTopic, a topic modeling technique that generates coherent topic representations. Then, topics obtained from the two sets of documents (research and policies) were compared. This study found that sustainability, life-cycle assessment, climate change, and ecosystem services are central research themes in the country. The topic modeling analysis showed that research has been concentrated on the areas of "Sustainable Fisheries and Aquaculture Management" and "Sustainable Bioenergy Production and Chemical Processes." The topics most frequently present in the environmental policies were "Environmental Education for Children: Awareness | 3 of 35 RESEARCH FUNDING AND ENVIRONMENTAL POLICIES

involved with research organizations should be motivated to contribute to policy development and evaluation because research helps make better policies (Siar, 2023). Moreover, research findings must be relevant and more accessible to policy makers (Prosser et al., 2013). Hence, the research-policy interface includes a reflection on research priorities and research organization (van den Hove, 2007).

Researchers and policy makers get frustrated with the mismatch between respective priorities and expectations for research (Prosser et al., 2013), which raises concerns about the impacts of policy on research and practice. Mejía-Cáceres et al. ( 2021) argued that researchers need to take into account the relationships between academia and policy making. Reed and Meagher (2022) stated that researchers are increasingly integrating and synthesizing evidence from different studies to propose more generalizable recommendations for policy making. Turnheim et al. ( 2020) stated that environment agencies need to engage with researchers to support sustainable transitions and that more approaches are required to increase the engagement between research and policy. Finally, Louder et al. ( 2021) consider that evaluating the impacts of environmental research on policy is challenging.

Despite the positive outcomes of a clear interface between research and policy and calls for stronger connections between policy and research (Prosser et al., 2013), policy makers consider that research findings are seldom utilized to determine or guide actions in policy making (La Brooy & Kelaher, 2017). Wehrens (2014) considers it relevant to empirically investigate how the domains of research and policy are similar or distinctive in some contexts. Strategies designed to increase the alignment between research and policy require a better comprehension of the research-policy interface (Redman et al., 2015).

The production of research aimed at improving social and environmental well-being depends on public funding (Arnott, 2021) and it has a key role in developing understanding of sustainability issues (Perrin et al., 2021). In fact, the promotion of sustainability transitions has received increasing attention both in the policy arena and in social science research (Markard et al., 2012). Policy is considered a component of sustainability transitions that can accelerate them (Edmondson et al., 2019). Indeed, there have been calls that policy should take environmental and social sustainability at its core (Kivimaa & Rogge, 2022). Since several research studies are carried out with resources from public funding agencies, it is essential to assess whether governments have been supporting research that can potentially contribute and guide future environmental policies and whether research has been supporting the elaboration of public policies.

However, we know very little about funders' goals, despite wider calls to produce actionable knowledge for sustainability (Arnott, 2021). Understanding how policy making can influence the rate and direction of sustainability transitions is an important, yet underexplored research stream (Edmondson et al., 2019). Within the context of sustainability, funding agencies are understudied even though they have long been known to research policy scholars as influential actors. Exploring this subject further can promote practical knowledge in sustainability (Arnott, 2021). Finally, there is a call for more thorough evaluations of policy support mechanisms and balanced research in terms of geographical coverage (Turnheim et al., 2020).

In order to fill these research gaps, the objective of this study was to assess the researchpolicy interface in the environmental area by identifying similarities and gaps between science and policy. In order to do so, a bibliometric analysis was conducted on a set of related research articles published with recognized acknowledgments to important research agencies in Chile. In addition, with the aid of advanced topic modeling techniques, we identified the gaps, considering frequent topics, between public policy documents and scientific studies to guide future research and policy making.

paragraphs: Research is critical to maintain and improve growth, welfare, and international competitiveness (Bloch et al., 2014). To achieve these objectives, researchers depend on public resources, which govern research based on guidelines provided by policy makers and funding agencies (Sauermann et al., 2020). Moreover, researchers objectively assess the outcomes of public policies. Hence, a clear interface between research (science) and policy, in which research and policy creation are strategically influenced by each other is expected (Schut et al., 2013).

The science-policy interface is defined by van den Hove (2007, p. 807) as "social processes encompassing the relations between scientists and other actors in the policy process, and which allows for exchanges, co-evolution, and joint construction of knowledge with the aim of enriching decision-making." When research and policy have a unified interface, scientific interests and policy priorities share similar goals (Siar, 2023), its barriers are known, and possible cooperation strategies are identified (Jansen et al., 2012). A clear and fructiferous research-policy interface provides an opportunity to advocate for more research investment, allows research funding bodies to evaluate which projects have more impact, and increases the likelihood of practical application of research outcomes (Ritter & Lancaster, 2013).

Research and policy are intersecting domains of human activity (van den Hove, 2007). Hawkins and Parkhurst (2016) stated that solid research evidence should support policy measures. Scientists and Care," "Bird Conservation and Habitat Importance in Chile," and "National Biodiversity Conservation and Sustainable Use Strategy." The greatest gaps are observed in the clusters of "Sustainable Bioenergy Production and Chemical Processes" and "Copper Mining and Mineral Processing Techniques" (prevalent in research) and in "Anatomy and Physiology of Aquatic and Terrestrial Organisms" and "Biogeography and Taxonomy of Southern Hemisphere Insects" (prevalent in policy).

This study has important contributions to scholars, practitioners, and policy makers in the field. First, this study presents a novel technique to analyze the interface between research and environmental public policies. Second, this study helps funding agencies and researchers, especially in Chile and other emerging economies, by presenting a novel process to evaluate how research and public policies are related. Third, this study also proposes a research agenda to promote better alignment with recently published environmental policies. Finally, this study contributes to the important and necessary demonstration and justification of public funding received by research studies. By analyzing the link between public policies and research, this study calls attention to the fact that policy makers and funding agencies should further encourage and support projects that address sustainability problems and require external support.

sections.header_raw: Policy-making processes and environmental policy research
sections.header_norm: policy making processes and environmental policy research
paragraphs: Policy making involves prioritizing, planning, implementing, and evaluating policy initiatives in a process that is influenced by different actors (Loncarevic et al., 2021). In all these phases, science can contribute to policy development, and policy can provide inputs and requirements for new research. Then, the interfaces between science and policy provide policy makers with knowledge, scientific outcomes, and co-production processes to support informed decisionmaking and sustainable development (Wagner et al., 2023;Wang et al., 2024). Especially in developed economies, governments rely on scientific evidence to design public policies, which increases interest in the links between policy making and scientific knowledge (Reichmann & Wieser, 2022). However, although research is frequently carried out to support policy making, several research results are not accessible to the policy domain (Schut et al., 2013). Researchers have been concerned about how to translate research findings into knowledge for policy (Pirrone et al., 2022), reducing the gap between evidence and policy (Reichmann & Wieser, 2022).

Policy instruments support the definition and achievement of policy goals and are elaborated through constant discussions with different stakeholders (Bali et al., 2021). They are part of the policy-making process and represent the ways through which policy makers attempt to solve policy problems (Capano, 2024). Public research funding is one kind of policy instrument since it is a mechanism through which governments direct public resources to develop research to support solutions to a country's problems. Funding agencies have a substantial influence on funding calls, proposal evaluation, and funding allocation. In this context, connecting the technical and political aspects of research funding is essential to support policy making and its objectives (Larrue et al., 2018). Thus, research funding agencies are relevant actors in the research-policy interface. They have the potential to scientifically support informed policy making by connecting scientists with policy makers and directing incentives to credible, relevant, and legitimate research (Ojanen et al., 2021).

Environmental policy can be defined as "the set of tools, techniques, programs, instruments, plans, and ideas associated with the governance of ecosystems and their interactions with society" (Pacheco-Vega, 2021, p. 388). They encourage or limit production and consumption behavior toward environmental outcomes (Jacob & Ekins, 2020) and are one of the primary mechanisms affecting subsidies and firms' investment in environmentally oriented actions (Barbosa, 2024;Ji et al., 2024). The consequences of environmental policies extend into several sectors of the economy (Konisky & Woods, 2012).

Environmental policies are complex instruments that comprise several dimensions, venues, and stakeholders (Ellis et al., 2020). In order to endure, international commitments, public administration, and preimplementation processes involving different actors are required (Koskimaa et al., 2021). In addition, the successful implementation of environmental policies relies on the State's institutional capacity and the presence of solid policy structures, political support (Melidis & Russel, 2020), administrative intervention, and mandatory implementation (Shi et al., 2023). These policies are also affected by political pressures (Konisky & Woods, 2012) and societal demands. Given the increasing environmental pressures and the complexity of the world's environmental challenges, environmental policies have become increasingly essential (Bayarsaikhan et al., 2023). They have also been intensely debated by politicians, who are pressured by society (Koskimaa et al., 2021).

Environmental policies present significant effects on production and operational procedures, yielding several benefits, such as a significant reduction in ecological footprint (Appiah et al., 2023), energy efficiency promotion, energy conservation, greenhouse gas (GHG) emissions reduction, increases in investments for renewable energy (Ji et al., 2024;Shi et al., 2023), landfill waste mitigation, improvement of waste management and recycling practices, reduction in resource depletion (Meleddu et al., 2024;Ogieriakhi & Wang, 2024), green innovation, and technology adoption (Gugler et al., 2024). On the other hand, environmental policies define constraints and incentives that may increase production costs, reduce production, influence innovation investments, and limit firms' environmental strategies (Kim et al., 2024).

paragraphs: Environmental policies and their objectives vary from country to country (Bayarsaikhan et al., 2023). In Chile, environmental policies were influenced by the transition to democracy in 1990. Since 1990, the environment has been considered a question of public policy, and objectives related to sustainable development, environment protection, and the health of the population have been established. At this time, gradualism was established as an important principle that involved continuous improvement of the established policies (del Campo & Lukic, 2020).

In 1994, a General Environmental Law (Ley 19,300) established the country's first comprehensive environmental governance framework and created the National Commission on the Environment (CONAMA), an administrative office whose function was to advise the president in related matters (Velasco & Mumme, 2021). The National System of Environmental Information (SINIA) and the Environmental Impact Assessment Service (SEIA) were also created. Public participation in environmental assessment processes was allowed (Velasco & Mumme, 2021). Finally, a regime of environmental responsibility was established (del Campo & Lukic, 2020).

SEIA was the primary instrument for environmental management. It defines that all investment projects impacting the environment must go through the system to obtain the corresponding permitting. Projects with strong impacts must submit a rigorous, full-scale environmental impact study, including mitigation and compensation strategies (Silva, 2023). Project owners must present an environmental impact declaration or carry out an environmental impact study. Then, public institutions determine whether the project fulfills the criteria and requirements to be approved. For some time, SEIA has been criticized because it was considered the only environmental policy instrument and it was used beyond its responsibilities in terms of policy and regulation (del Campo & Lukic, 2020).

Criticism increased the intensity of the debate on the appropriate model for environmental policy. This debate culminated in the creation of the Ministry of the Environment in 2010 by President Michele Bachelet's, replacing the CONAMA with the new ministry, which was considered at that time with limited regulatory capacity (del Campo & Lukic, 2020;Silva, 2023). The same law that created the Ministry of the Environment also established the Environmental Assessment Service and the Superintendency of the Environment. A few years later, in 2012, Law 20,600 was enacted during the government of President Sebastián Piñera (2010-2014), creating the Environmental Courts. Currently, the three courts contemplated by the legislation operate geographically distributed in the north, center, and south of the country (Carrasco & Maillet, 2019;Silva, 2023).

Chile's environmental framework complies with multilateral conventions and addresses the environmental standards of trade partners (Velasco & Mumme, 2021). Figure 1 shows the current institutional environmental framework.

Figure 1 depicts the Biodiversity and Protected Areas Service, included in a modification of Law No. 20,417. However, this modification has yet to be approved by the Chamber of Deputies. The purpose of this service is to support biodiversity conversation and the administration of a unique national system of protected areas (Orrego et al., 2023).

The country still faces the challenge of strengthening its legal framework as well as developing its organizational capacity to address the potentially significant environmental impacts of large projects and industrial activities (Nasirov et al., 2018). However, modifications have been made to the country's bureaucratic infrastructure, positively impacting transparency and public participation in the matter (Ghorbani & Kuan, 2017). In 2017, Chile ratified the Paris Agreement and committed to developing policies on climate change and to achieve sustainable development objectives (Simsek et al., 2019). In Chile, between 0.36% and 0.7% of the gross domestic product (GDP) has been spent on research and development (R&D) in recent years, which is low when compared to developed countries where often more than 2% of GDP is devoted to R&D (Dobrzanski, 2020;Ubfal & Maffioli, 2011). In Chile, R&D expenditure have been focused mainly on machinery, software, and equipment purchasing, becoming key to stimulating the R&D activity and generating sustained economic growth (Mardones & Velásquez, 2021).

Environmental policies and their objectives vary from country to country (Bayarsaikhan et al., 2023). In Chile, environmental policies were influenced by the transition to democracy in 1990. Since 1990, the environment has been considered a question of public policy, and objectives related to sustainable development, environment protection, and the health of the population have been established. At this time, gradualism was established as an important principle that involved continuous improvement of the established policies (del Campo & Lukic, 2020).

In 1994, a General Environmental Law (Ley 19,300) established the country's first comprehensive environmental governance framework and created the National Commission on the Environment (CONAMA), an administrative office whose function was to advise the president in related matters (Velasco & Mumme, 2021). The National System of Environmental Information (SINIA) and the Environmental Impact Assessment Service (SEIA) were also created. Public participation in environmental assessment processes was allowed (Velasco & Mumme, 2021). Finally, a regime of environmental responsibility was established (del Campo & Lukic, 2020).

SEIA was the primary instrument for environmental management. It defines that all investment projects impacting the environment must go through the system to obtain the corresponding permitting. Projects with strong impacts must submit a rigorous, full-scale environmental impact study, including mitigation and compensation strategies (Silva, 2023). Project owners must present an environmental impact declaration or carry out an environmental impact study. Then, public institutions determine whether the project fulfills the criteria and requirements to be approved. For some time, SEIA has been criticized because it was considered the only environmental policy instrument and it was used beyond its responsibilities in terms of policy and regulation (del Campo & Lukic, 2020).

Criticism increased the intensity of the debate on the appropriate model for environmental policy. This debate culminated in the creation of the Ministry of the Environment in 2010 by President Michele Bachelet's, replacing the CONAMA with the new ministry, which was considered at that time with limited regulatory capacity (del Campo & Lukic, 2020;Silva, 2023). The same law that created the Ministry of the Environment also established the Environmental Assessment Service and the Superintendency of the Environment. A few years later, in 2012, Law 20,600 was enacted during the government of President Sebastián Piñera (2010-2014), creating the Environmental Courts. Currently, the three courts contemplated by the legislation operate geographically distributed in the north, center, and south of the country (Carrasco & Maillet, 2019;Silva, 2023).

Chile's environmental framework complies with multilateral conventions and addresses the environmental standards of trade partners (Velasco & Mumme, 2021). Figure 1 shows the current institutional environmental framework.

Figure 1 depicts the Biodiversity and Protected Areas Service, included in a modification of Law No. 20,417. However, this modification has yet to be approved by the Chamber of Deputies. The purpose of this service is to support biodiversity conversation and the administration of a unique national system of protected areas (Orrego et al., 2023).

The country still faces the challenge of strengthening its legal framework as well as developing its organizational capacity to address the potentially significant environmental impacts of large projects and industrial activities (Nasirov et al., 2018). However, modifications have been made to the country's bureaucratic infrastructure, positively impacting transparency and public participation in the matter (Ghorbani & Kuan, 2017). sections.paper_id: Barbosa and Gomes - 2026 - The interface between research funding and environmental policies in an emergent economy using neura
sections.header_norm: funded research and research policy interface
paragraphs: Project grants given by funding institutions are designed to support research (Boyle, 2020). Funding agencies support the development of science by providing financial resources beyond the funding usually offered by universities and research organizations. The process of granting research funds is usually competitive, and the results affect both researchers and the whole research system (Möller, 2019). Academic institutions have been pressured to secure external funding, and the capacity to obtain these grants has been considered a key metric of academic assessment (Rau et al., 2018). Funding agencies, in turn, attempt to establish strong relationships with grantees in order to maximize the projects' return on investment (Sambrano & Millan, 2020).

Previous studies have analyzed the benefits and positive impacts caused by research grants. Funding grants are expected to produce high-quality research, impact the researcher's and the institution's reputation, and increase networking and collaboration (Bloch et al., 2014). In fact, there are funding programs specifically aimed at supporting scientific research and fostering research collaboration (Tamtik & Sá, 2012;Ubfal & Maffioli, 2011;Zhao, 2010). Funding is also correlated with other outcomes, such as patents, entrepreneurship, commercial products, and consulting agreements (Gulbrandsen & Smeby, 2005). In addition, funded studies are more likely to be cited above average (Álvarez-bornstein & Bordons, 2021;Rigby, 2013), showing higher impacts in terms of journal ranking and citation counts than research that is not sponsored (Wang & Shapira, 2015). Therefore, funding has a significant direct influence on researchers' productivity (Defazio et al., 2009) since the quality of their research may be improved because of more time available to do research and due to the possibility of hiring research assistants and gaining access to better data and technology (Zhao, 2010).

Recently, impact assessments of funding grants have become more common since funding agencies need to demonstrate that these grants have generated the expected impacts on research performance and scientific quality (Langfeldt et al., 2015). In fact, some agencies, in order to be able to evidence the ROI of their projects, may stipulate guidelines and constraints referring to the assessment of studies' impact on science and society (Rau et al., 2018).

Studies that attempt to support the characterization and evaluation of funded research usually analyze funding acknowledgment data published in research papers (Rigby, 2013). Acknowledgments may express gratitude to individuals, institutions, or research agencies that have somehow supported the study, as well as formal recognition to research funders (Álvarez-Bornstein et al., 2017). The inclusion of research funding data in the acknowledgments sections of research papers in bibliographic databases has contributed to the increased number of studies that analyze this type of data (Rigby, 2011). Funding acknowledgment information identifies organizations that support research projects and their related publications (Rigby, 2013). Also, an increasing demand for the expansion of research funding and the assessment of the impacts of these investments has been recently observed (Wang & Shapira, 2015).

Government institutions and funding agencies agree that a research policy is critical to maintaining and improving growth and international competitiveness (Bloch et al., 2014). Hence, government and funding institutions must work together to define how investments in research and development should be made to deliver outcomes, insights, and tools to the challenges a country faces. Consequently, it is essential to assess how public investments have been made and if there is a clear link between research and policy making, especially in the context of environmental policy. Environmental policies are a way that governments have to intervene in environmental issues through laws and regulations to improve the diffusion of cleaner technologies and promote sustainable transitions (Chen et al., 2022).

Thus, research and policy can be interconnected in several ways, described by different models and approaches. The first type of possible connection is the notion that knowledge and ideas shape policy. It may work if there is good communication and trust between researchers and policy makers. The second type of connection assumes that policy shapes research. Hence, politics and power relations shape and constrain research and its use, dictating knowledge production and utilization. The third type of research-policy interface is coproduction, which posits that research and policy are mutually constitutive and research and that policy reinforce each other. It is seen as a more positive way of demonstrating politics' influence on research in that knowledge production and use are considered inherent parts of governance (Boswell & Smith, 2017;Siar, 2023).

Another model that describes research typology is Murray's model ( 2011), which considers three interaction possibilities to describe researcher-policymaker involvement in policy implementation: the Customer/client, interactive model, and joint construction. According to Siar (2023), other approaches to represent and describe the interface between research and policy have also been proposed. The pyramid approach assumes three levels of interface (awareness, influence, and impact), the ultimate level of which is achieved when research is applied in policy making. The influencing approach considers the possible impacts of policy (attitudinal, behavior, procedures, policy content, and discursive commitment). Finally, the results chain approach tracks policy influence using the results chain (inputs, activities, outputs, outcomes, and impacts) to achieve the desired objectives. In this study, we take Murray's view of a joint construction, assuming that research and policy can mutually influence each other.

sections.paper_id: Barbosa and Gomes - 2026 - The interface between research funding and environmental policies in an emergent economy using neura
paragraphs: This study was conducted in two phases. The first phase comprised retrieving and analyzing research papers supported by Chilean research funding agencies on subjects related to sustainability with the aim of better comprehending the research topics investigated in these papers. The second phase comprised the analysis of the similarities/gaps between the environmental public policies and research retrieved in the first phase. Neural topic modeling techniques were employed to perform this activity. These procedures are explained in further detail as follows.

sections.paper_id: Barbosa and Gomes - 2026 - The interface between research funding and environmental policies in an emergent economy using neura
paragraphs: To construct the dataset for the bibliometric phase of this study, papers were retrieved from the Web of Science (WoS) database, which covers a considerable range of high-ranking journals and high-quality peer-reviewed articles (Skute, 2019). This methodological approach has been adopted in previous research (Ferramosca & Verona, 2020). Despite its quality, the WoS database was chosen since it contains information on funding acknowledgments (Álvarez-Bornstein et al., 2017) and because the accuracy of funding information collected by WoS is considered superior when compared to Scopus. The Scopus database was not included since it still contains some errors in funding acknowledgment data and it needs to improve the text identification and extraction methods for acknowledgment information (Liu, 2020).

A research study is funded when explicit acknowledgment of the support of a research sponsor or grant award to develop the research is presented (Álvarez-bornstein & Bordons, 2021). The WoS database contains specific fields with funding acknowledgment data that allow the identification of the funding organization and the grant number for the corresponding research project (Möller, 2019). In this study, sponsoring organizations were identified by the "funding agency" field.

In order to retrieve research studies, a search string was executed in the WoS database. The terms of the search string were defined based on the main subject analyzed in this study, sustainability (Barbosa, 2021;Birkel & Müller, 2021;Fobbe & Hilletofth, 2021), as well as on names of funding agencies in Chile. Thus, the following search string was used as research topic: (sustainab* OR "corporate social responsibility" OR csr OR "corporate sustainability" OR "Triple Bottom Line" OR "Circular Economy"), and the following terms were used for funding agencies: (ANID OR "Agencia Nacional de Investigación y Desarrollo" OR CONICYT OR "Comisión Nacional de Investigación Científica y Tecnlógica").

National The second phase of this study consisted of the analysis of environmental policies on sustainability. The public policies related to the subject of this study were obtained from the website of the Ministry of the Environment. These documents were selected based on the following contents and criteria: (1) policies relevant to sustainability; (2) policies that present clear objectives and action plans; and (3) policies with a national coverage. The 16 policy documents analyzed in this study are presented in Appendix 1.

In this study, we follow researchers who consider that research and policies should be jointly constructed. Mitchell et al. (2009) acknowledge that the research-policy relationship is based on a bidirectional flow of information and knowledge between actors. Murray (2011) defends twoway communication between researchers and policy makers. Black (2001) states that the linear relationship between research and policy must be replaced by a more interactive model that facilitates obtaining information and fostering stakeholder involvement.

We also agree with Siar (2023) who advocates that measuring the influence of research on policy is challenging and not straightforward due to the complexity and nonlinearity of the policy processes, the number of stakeholders involved, the gradual nature of this influence, and the time lag between research investment and impact. Boswell and Smith (2017) suggest that the influence of research on policy making is not immediate and direct. In addition, policy makers tend to make late connections with researchers (La Brooy & Kelaher, 2017).

Despite these challenges, we follow Siar ( 2023), who stated that policy influence cannot be attributed to one study alone, but rather to the joint impact of several related research documents. Hence, in order to overcome the challenge of determining a time frame to analyze the researchpolicy interface, we considered that both sets of documents (policies and research) should have been published in the same period, which was defined from 2015 to 2023 due to the publication date of the set of analyzed public policies.

We also agree with Siar (2023) who advocates that measuring the influence of research on policy is challenging and not straightforward due to the complexity and nonlinearity of the policy processes, the number of stakeholders involved, the gradual nature of this influence, and the time lag between research investment and impact. Boswell and Smith (2017) suggest that the influence of research on policy making is not immediate and direct. In addition, policy makers tend to make late connections with researchers (La Brooy & Kelaher, 2017).

Despite these challenges, we follow Siar ( 2023), who stated that policy influence cannot be attributed to one study alone, but rather to the joint impact of several related research documents. Hence, in order to overcome the challenge of determining a time frame to analyze the researchpolicy interface, we considered that both sets of documents (policies and research) should have been published in the same period, which was defined from 2015 to 2023 due to the publication date of the set of analyzed public policies.
sections.preview_head: To construct the dataset for the bibliometric phase of this study, papers were retrieved from the Web of Science (WoS) database, which covers a considerable range of high-ranking journals and high-quality peer-reviewed articles (Skute, 2019). This methodological approach has been adopted in previous research (Ferramosca & Verona, 2020). Despite its quality, the WoS database was chosen since it contains information on funding acknowledgments (Álvarez-Bornstein et al., 2017) and because the accuracy of funding information collected by WoS is considered superior when compared to Scopus.

paragraphs: For the bibliometric analysis, an amount of 1268 unique articles were found after executing the search string in the WoS database. After analyzing the title, abstract, and other parts of the papers, whenever necessary, 489 were removed since they were not related to the main theme of this study. So, this study analyzed a set of 779 articles. Therefore, a quantitative bibliometric study was considered suitable since it can examine this amount of data, which would be timeconsuming with the adoption of other methods.

This study conducted the bibliometrics analysis using the bibliometrix R package (Aria & Cuccurullo, 2017), designed for quantitative research in bibliometrics. Additionally, biblioshiny, an app providing a web interface for bibliometrix, was used for visualizing bibliometric data. Bibliometrix provides different functions to support a typical bibliometric workflow (Della Corte et al., 2019). Then, the dataset was used to construct a structural topic model (STM), as described in the following subsection.

paragraphs: Topic models have emerged as pivotal tools in the examination of intricate social phenomena, serving as a conduit between the domains of social science and (un)structured analysis, various modes of logical reasoning, and the field of big data analytics (Hannigan et al., 2019). They offer a robust statistical technique for identifying hidden themes within a corpus of documents, revealing underlying patterns and relationships in raw data (Prollochs & Feuerriegel, 2020).

BERTopic is a topic modeling technique that leverages clustering methodologies and a classbased variation of term frequency-inverse document frequency (TF-IDF) to generate coherent topic representations (Grootendorst, 2022). This model encompasses three independent stages: (1) creating document embeddings using pretrained language models, (2) reducing the dimensionality of these embeddings to form semantically similar clusters of documents, and (3) developing a class-based version of TF-IDF to extract topic representation from each topic. These steps can be seen in detail in Figure 2. This flexible structure makes BERTopic applicable to different contexts (Grootendorst, 2022).

To encode semantic similarity in vector space, BERTopic employs the Sentence-BERT (SBERT) framework (Reimers & Gurevych, 2019) to create document embeddings. SBERT enables the transformation of sentences and paragraphs into dense vector representations using pretrained language models, which result in semantically meaningful sentence embeddings that are better suited for tasks like semantic similarity search and clustering (Cheng et al., 2023). Managing data in high dimensions often poses difficulties, mainly attributed to the curse of dimensionality (Aggarwal et al., 2002). To address this problem, dimensionality reduction techniques such as uniform manifold approximation and projection (UMAP) are utilized. UMAP helps maintain both the local and global properties of the data while representing it in reduced dimensions (McInnes et al., 2018). The dimensionally reduced data points are then clustered using the Hierarchical Density-Based Spatial Clustering of Applications with Noise (HDBSCAN) algorithm (McInnes et al., 2017). This technique improves upon DBSCAN by handling clusters with different densities and efficiently separating outliers. By leveraging the hierarchical framework created by HDBSCAN, it is possible to adeptly pinpoint and isolate clusters and outliers (Stewart & Al-Khassaweneh, 2022).

The topic representations are modeled based on the documents in each cluster, where each cluster represents one topic. TF-IDF, traditionally used to represent the importance of a word to a document, is modified to represent a term's importance to a topic instead. This is achieved by treating all documents in a cluster as a single document and adjusting TF-IDF accordingly. Inverse document frequency is replaced by inverse class frequency to measure a term's information contribution to a class. Consequently, the importance of words in clusters is modeled, rather than in individual documents, generating topic word distributions for each cluster. Finally, by merging the class-based TF-IDF (c-TF-IDF) representations of the least common topic with its most similar counterpart, the number of topics can be reduced to a user-specified value (Grootendorst, 2022).

In contrast to other methods, BERTopic operates exceptionally well with pretrained embeddings due to the segregation between document clustering and the use of c-TF-IDF to extract topic representations (Sánchez-Franco & Rey-Moreno, 2022). Notably, this approach using c-TF-IDF has shown to be effective for topic modeling (Zhou et al., 2022). This procedure allows BERTopic to accommodate various topic modeling variants, such as guided topic modeling, dynamic topic modeling, or class-based topic modeling (Egger & Yu, 2022).

The c-TF-IDF procedure yields candidate topics, each composed of a distinct set of keywords and associated representative documents, which can be fine-tuned to optimize topic representations. This methodology, with its unique feature of providing a specific set of representative documents for each topic, significantly reduces computational requirements by allowing fine tuning to operate on a constrained dataset. Consequently, the use of large language models becomes practically feasible in production settings. This approach enhances the efficiency of the topic modeling process, ensuring rigorous topic representation (BERTopic, 2023).

GPT-4, a sizable generative model, can be used to construct these representations. Such models can be used in diverse applications, including dialogue systems, text summarization, and machine translation. GPT-4 demonstrates performance comparable to human expertise across most professional and academic examinations, validating the model's capabilities and highlighting its potential in various high-stakes scenarios, ranging from professional applications to academic assessments (OpenAI, 2023).

sections.header_raw: Identification of topics and semantical comparison of research and environmental policies
sections.header_norm: identification of topics and semantical comparison of research and environmental policies
paragraphs: In this study, a dataset of 16 public policy documents was examined. These documents were converted from PDF to TXT format using ABBYY FineReader and then translated from Spanish to English with Google Translate. Additionally, the abstracts of the set of 779 articles were analyzed. These abstracts, along with the translated State documents, were used for topic identification via clustering techniques. Prioritizing paper abstracts over full-text content is due to data availability and relative ease in extending our proposed methodological approach to other potential fields of knowledge as recommended by Cheng et al. (2018). The abstracts and government documents were segmented into sentences using the Spacy library, resulting in a total of 29,325 sentences. Of these, 6937 belonged to the scientific articles, while 22,388 were from government documents. Sentence clustering was performed using BERTopic, which used the "all-mpnet-base-v2" model for generating sentence embeddings due to its superior performance (SBERT.net, 2023).

The minimum topic size was determined to be 49 sentences. The clustering size was decided based on a kernel density function that measures the density connections between pairs of objects to assess the connectedness within and between clusters. The application of this method was grounded on the work by Moulavi et al. (2014), which used the density-based clustering validation (DBCV) to determine optimal cluster and sample sizes. Following their methodology, we established the minimum topic size required for effective clustering sentences to be 49. In the end, 104 topics were identified. These outliers were not primarily associated with any specific topic. The presence of a high number of outliers is common when using BERTopic. Previous researchers have pointed out that it generates many outliers, which is a current limitation of the algorithm (Egger & Yu, 2022). When generating the representative keywords for the topics, stop words were removed using the English words list from Natural Language Toolkit (NLTK) (Loper & Bird, 2002). Then, the ChatGPT API, GPT-4, was employed to generate labels for these topics, guided by a prompt that considered both the keywords and representative documents extracted from BERTopic. The extraction of the topic label followed the format:

Of the original 104 topics, 11 were eliminated because they did not refer to environmental themes. Some of the eliminated topics are "Academic Citation and Authorship Patterns," "Bibliometric and Content Analysis of Scientific Literature," and "Publication Copyrights and Years." In the end, 93 clusters were considered. Then, the count of sentences for each topic within the scientific articles and government documents was calculated. These data were normalized by dividing the sentence count by the total number of sentences in its respective category, yielding a percentage of sentences covering a specific topic within each category. This allowed for a comparative analysis of the prevalence of specific topics within scientific literature and governmental documents. As suggested by Cheng et al. (2018), combining topic modeling methods with bibliometric data can assist in fully explaining research concerns and support future investigations.

sections.header_norm: bibliometric analysis characterization of research studies
paragraphs: This section shows the results of the bibliometric analysis performed in this study. As explained previously, 779 publications were retrieved on sustainability that were funded by public research agencies in Chile. Figure 3 presents the number of articles published between 2015 and 2023. A constant increase in the number of published studies can be observed in Figure 2.

The main venue for publication of funded research studies on sustainability in the country is the sustainability journal, by MDPI editorial, an open-access journal. Figure 4 shows the journal names where most studies were published.

We found that only a few authors have written most funded studies on the subject in Chile (Figure 5). This might show that training in how to apply for research funding is relatively limited (Boyle, 2020) and competencies related to writing winning grant projects take a long time to be developed. Moreover, winning projects are usually associated with experienced researchers in the field, and with a good track record in obtaining grant funding (Boyle, 2020). Previous researchers have found that seniority, funding and university's reputation are determinant factors that explain differences in research efficiency among scholars (Amara et al., 2020). Previous studies have shown that scientists at prestigious universities tend to have higher publication and citation rates, more research experience and competence, more familiarity with research processes and subjects, more graduate students to work on these projects, and a better capacity to raise resources than scholars at universities in lower academic ranks (Amara et al., 2020;Wohlrabe et al., 2019). Consequently, researchers at higher-ranked institutions benefit from "economies of scale" since they can optimize their resources.

We found that only a few authors have written most funded studies on the subject in Chile (Figure 5). This might show that training in how to apply for research funding is relatively limited (Boyle, 2020) and competencies related to writing winning grant projects take a long time to be developed. Moreover, winning projects are usually associated with experienced researchers in the field, and with a good track record in obtaining grant funding (Boyle, 2020). Previous researchers have found that seniority, funding and university's reputation are determinant factors that explain differences in research efficiency among scholars (Amara et al., 2020). Previous studies have shown that scientists at prestigious universities tend to have higher publication and citation rates, more research experience and competence, more familiarity with research processes and subjects, more graduate students to work on these projects, and a better capacity to raise resources than scholars at universities in lower academic ranks (Amara et al., 2020;Wohlrabe et al., 2019). Consequently, researchers at higher-ranked institutions benefit from "economies of scale" since they can optimize their resources.

paragraphs: In order to support the characterization of research themes, keywords were extracted from the author-supplied keyword list of all publications. Figure 6 shows a word cloud with the most frequent keywords supplied by authors. The most frequent words are management, sustainability, conservation, climate change, and performance.

Thematic maps organize research themes in different quadrants in terms of centrality and density. Biblioshiny takes clusters of keywords provided by authors and their interconnections to identify research themes, which are characterized by their density and centrality in the words' network. Density measures the cohesiveness among the words and is represented in the vertical axis. Centrality represents the degree of correlation among topics and is represented in the horizontal axis. These measures represent the importance a research theme has in the community and the theme's ability to develop and sustain itself (Agbo et al., 2021). Figure 7 shows the thematic map using authors' keywords to define research themes.

Themes found in the upper-right quadrant are called motor themes, that is, these are important topics for the research field. These themes are developed in the field and are essential for organizing the study topic (Khare & Jain, 2022;Wei & Jiang, 2023). Themes in the upper-left quadrant are niche themes (high density and low centrality). They are highly developed but of little relevance, indicating that they are not intrinsically related to mainstream research in the area (Wei & Jiang, 2023). They present established internal connections but their external links are negligible (Khare & Jain, 2022). Themes in the lower-left quadrant are known as declining or emergent themes (low density and low centrality); they are weakly developed and considered marginal by the research community. These themes are immature without a powerful central According to Figure 7, sustainability, life cycle assessment, developing countries, Chile, climate change, ecosystem services, urban planning, water scarcity, and agriculture represent motor themes of sustainability-funded research in Chile. Basic themes of sustainability research in the country include circular economy, energy efficiency, data envelopment analysis, sustainable development, solar energy, participation, renewable energy, rural electrification, and sustainable energy. "Cycling," "public transport," and "social sustainability" are classified as motor/ niche themes. Niche themes include battery energy storage and semi-autogenous grid. Declining themes comprise CO 2 emissions and energy nanomaterials.

As keywords represent a generalization of the topic in articles, the analysis of the keywords from a certain research field benefits the discovery of the research hotspots (Peng et al., 2020). Article keywords used by authors define topical aspects of a field and allow the identification of trending topics in terms of keywords occurrences (Agbo et al., 2021). The identification of research tendencies was performed by identifying trending keywords. These trending words represent terms that have been used as keywords. The identification of these words was done based on the authors' provided keyword list. Figure 8 displays the trending keywords, showing how frequently these words have been used during the previous years. The analysis of keywords identified some terms as trend research topics: waste management, management, and carbon footprint. Previously, in 2022, the following words were considered trend topics: circular economy, sustainable development goals, and life cycle assessment.

It is possible to observe that the topics identified as trend or hot research topics by the bibliometric analysis are related to the word clusters identified with topic modeling techniques.
The top 25 clusters considering the number of sentences in each cluster are presented in Table 1. The complete list of the 93 clusters can be found in a Supplementary File to this publication.

Table 2 displays the gap between research articles and government documents. In order to calculate the gap between the two sets of texts, the percentage of presence of each word cluster in each set of documents has been normalized regarding the number of sentences in each group of texts. Table 2 also shows in which set of documents the word cluster is more frequently observed. The greatest gaps are observed in the clusters of "Sustainable Bioenergy Production and Chemical Processes" and "Copper Mining and Mineral Processing Techniques" (prevalent themes in research) and in the clusters of "Anatomy and Physiology of Aquatic and Terrestrial Organisms," "Biogeography and Taxonomy of Southern Hemisphere Insects," "Astronomy and Observatories: Advancements in Telescopic Technology," and "Oceanic Islands and Marine Biodiversity of Chile" (prevalent themes in policy).

The themes with more similarities (smallest gaps) between research and policy documents are "Waste Management and Recycling in a Circular Economy," "Climate Patterns and Changes: El Niño Effects and Seasonal Variability," "Forest Management and Conservation Policies," and "GHG Emissions and Trends Across Sectors."

sections.paper_id: Barbosa and Gomes - 2026 - The interface between research funding and environmental policies in an emergent economy using neura
paragraphs: The general objective of this study was to evaluate the interface between research and policy in the area of sustainability through the identification of similarities and gaps between science and policy. This study was carried out in Chile. We identified the main research topics through a bibliometric analysis carried out on a set of articles published with recognized acknowledgments to important research agencies in the country. In addition, we applied topic modeling techniques to identify the main topics presented both in the environmental sustainability-related public policies and in the set of research articles. Then, we semantically compared both datasets. We also identified research gaps and suggestions for future research. This study proposes an innovative method to assess the interface between research and public policies in the area.

This study reinforces the importance of environmental bibliometric research, whose focus lies on important and contemporary issues (Mougenot & Doussoulin, 2021). Moreover, the use of funding acknowledgments data from WoS publications has been analyzed in previous environmental studies in specific areas such as climate change (Koier & Horlings, 2015). Our study extends previous literature by performing an analysis of research supported by public funds on sustainability in Chile and analyzing how the main topics of sustainability have been covered by these studies. This reinforces the importance of this study since sustainability research should be locally relevant (Perrin et al., 2021).

This study identified an exponential growth in the number of published funded research studies on sustainability in Chile. Most research papers were published in open-access journals. This result is aligned with studies carried out in other countries. In India, one-quarter of the research in the country are available as open access. The world average is around 30% (Pavan & Barbosa, 2018). This result corroborates that, in Chile, funding is necessary to support the publication of accessible research studies.

This study found out that, in Chile, sustainability, life cycle assessment, developing countries, Chile, climate change, and ecosystem services represent motor themes of sustainability-funded research in Chile. The topic modeling analysis showed that research has been concentrated on the areas of "Sustainable Fisheries and Aquaculture Management," "Sustainable Bioenergy

Scarcity, Use, and Recharge." The topics most frequently present in the environmental policies were "Environmental Education for Children: Awareness and Care." "Bird Conservation and Habitat Importance in Chile." "National Biodiversity Conservation and Sustainable Use Strategy," "GHG Emissions and Trends Across Sectors," and "Anatomy and Physiology of Aquatic and Terrestrial Organisms." The greatest gaps are observed in the clusters of "Sustainable Bioenergy Production and Chemical Processes" and "Copper Mining and Mineral Processing Techniques" (prevalent themes in research) and in the clusters of "Anatomy and Physiology of Aquatic and Terrestrial Organisms," "Biogeography and Taxonomy of Southern Hemisphere Insects," "Astronomy and Observatories: Advancements in Telescopic Technology," and "Oceanic Islands and Marine Biodiversity of Chile" (prevalent themes in policy).

This study used a novel methodological approach based on topic modeling to achieve its objectives, addressing calls made by other researchers to integrate multiple theoretical and methodological approaches and novel methods capable of dealing with larger sets of data and increase interfaces among actors and institutions responsible for environmental outcomes (Ruseva et al., 2019).

This study identified significant gaps between funded research and policy in Chile in themes like sustainable bioenergy production and copper mining and mineral processing techniques. These topics are prevalent in research. Our study corroborates previous research that stated that limited bioenergy development is due to limited policy support, public policy deficiencies, and absent regulation for waste management and energy recovery (Ludlow et al., 2021). Our study also supports the findings of Guzmán et al. (2023), who stated that there is a lack of mining policy in Chile and the calls of researchers for a comprehensive mineral policy that contributes to sustainable and economic development (González et al., 2023). The findings of our study are crucial for guiding future research and policy development in these areas.

Our study reported significant gaps between policy and funded research regarding themes related to the anatomy and physiology of aquatic and terrestrial organisms, taxonomy of insects, astronomy and observatories, oceanic islands, and marine biodiversity, which are prevalent in policy documents. Researchers stated that Chile has developed a thorough institutional policy framework for climate change mitigation and adaptation that includes measures for biodiversity adaptation in the aquaculture/marine sectors (Engler, 2024). Despite a solid policy framework on the matter, our study shows that more research is required on this subject. These findings are aligned with previous research that observed that the aquaculture and fisheries industries require a research agenda to respond to sustainable demands (Aguilera & Larraín, 2021). Researchers also call for a more critical assessment of the regulatory framework to promote the evolution and adaptation of the aquaculture sector (Engler, 2024) in order to preserve marine biodiversity. In terms of astronomy and observatories, despite the development of several policy reforms to foster installation of observatories, there is still need for further qualitative and quantitative research in the area (Guridi et al., 2020), which confirms our study's findings.

The gaps identified in our study serve as a call to action for policy makers and scholars to consider the need for fostering research and policy development. Research funding agencies can use our study's findings to guide project calls to areas that require further investigation. Our study identified prevalent topics in environmental policies that have not been sufficiently explored by researchers, suggesting that related policies may not have been adequately assessed and that improvements to these policies may not have been identified based on reliable scientific methods. However, it is equally important for policy makers to be alert to areas that have been investigated by scholars but lack corresponding policies. Moreover. the topics our study identified, such as gaps in policies related to copper mining and bioenergy production, are crucial for the country's environmental and economic development.

This study also addresses calls to support research whose findings will be regarded by decision-makers in policy, planning, and public administration (Newig & Rose, 2020) and will offer evidence-based suggestions to improve the regulatory structure and stakeholder engagement (Villacura et al., 2024). A more intense link between research and policy will support overcoming the challenges related to environmental issues (Vargas et al., 2022).

This study also extends previous research made in Chile, a country where the pressure on the use of natural resources has greatly increased in recent years (Carranza et al., 2020). Velasco and Mumme (2021) stated that resource-intensive development and socio-environmental tensions are the country's most pressing environmental problems. According to researchers, the country's main current environmental challenges include accelerated GHG emissions, increased waste production, high atmospheric pollution, climate change, and air and water quality (Velasco & Mumme, 2021). Vargas et al. (2022) identified several action priorities related to environmental public policy for Latin America. Several of them are related to the research and policy topics identified in this study, comprising policies related to climate change, land and natural resources use, protection of biodiversity, extractive industries, carbon markets, renovation of soils, GHG emissions, and protected areas. Our study extends research focused on Chile and Latin America by identifying gaps between research and policy that could be addressed by future studies.

Our study showed that the interest in environmental themes related to copper mining is prevalent in research. It is known that copper exports represent around a third of Chilean GDP and that mining has had an important role in tax revenue generation (Mardones & del Rio, 2019). It is known that most of Chile's copper reserves are in highly water-stressed regions (Fitzsimons & Warren, 2024), which corroborates the need to carry out research focused on developing more environment-friendly exploration techniques. Our study also extends previous bibliometric research that has identified research trends in biogas technology in rural development (Tundup et al., 2021). Finally, our study also found that themes related to biodiversity conservation should be further investigated by researchers. Policies must regulate productive activities to reduce their impact on biodiversity.

Finally, this study calls our attention to strengthening the interactions within the complex environmental governance system formed by different actors and institutions in which procedures and policies support decision-making to safeguard the environment. In this system, the outcomes of research and policy will make stakeholders reflect on the need for different bodies and structures to implement and adjust policies. This system needs to be in constant improvement in order to optimize resources and reduce the time needed to take action based on research and policy outcomes. Thus, in order to implement an environmental agenda, those different stakeholders need to interact and jointly create policy and research in a timely manner.

Finally, this study calls our attention to strengthening the interactions within the complex environmental governance system formed by different actors and institutions in which procedures and policies support decision-making to safeguard the environment. In this system, the outcomes of research and policy will make stakeholders reflect on the need for different bodies and structures to implement and adjust policies. This system needs to be in constant improvement in order to optimize resources and reduce the time needed to take action based on research and policy outcomes. Thus, in order to implement an environmental agenda, those different stakeholders need to interact and jointly create policy and research in a timely manner.

The general objective of this study was to evaluate the interface between research and policy in the area of sustainability through the identification of similarities and gaps between science and policy. This study was carried out in Chile. We identified the main research topics through a bibliometric analysis carried out on a set of articles published with recognized acknowledgments to important research agencies in the country. In addition, we applied topic modeling techniques to identify the main topics presented both in the environmental sustainability-related public policies and in the set of research articles. Then, we semantically compared both datasets. We also identified research gaps and suggestions for future research. This study proposes an innovative method to assess the interface between research and public policies in the area.

This study reinforces the importance of environmental bibliometric research, whose focus lies on important and contemporary issues (Mougenot & Doussoulin, 2021). Moreover, the use of funding acknowledgments data from WoS publications has been analyzed in previous environmental studies in specific areas such as climate change (Koier & Horlings, 2015). Our study extends previous literature by performing an analysis of research supported by public funds on sustainability in Chile and analyzing how the main topics of sustainability have been covered by these studies. This reinforces the importance of this study since sustainability research should be locally relevant (Perrin et al., 2021).

This study identified an exponential growth in the number of published funded research studies on sustainability in Chile. Most research papers were published in open-access journals. This result is aligned with studies carried out in other countries. In India, one-quarter of the research in the country are available as open access. The world average is around 30% (Pavan & Barbosa, 2018). This result corroborates that, in Chile, funding is necessary to support the publication of accessible research studies.

This study found out that, in Chile, sustainability, life cycle assessment, developing countries, Chile, climate change, and ecosystem services represent motor themes of sustainability-funded research in Chile. The topic modeling analysis showed that research has been concentrated on the areas of "Sustainable Fisheries and Aquaculture Management," "Sustainable Bioenergy

This study used a novel methodological approach based on topic modeling to achieve its objectives, addressing calls made by other researchers to integrate multiple theoretical and methodological approaches and novel methods capable of dealing with larger sets of data and increase interfaces among actors and institutions responsible for environmental outcomes (Ruseva et al., 2019).

This study identified significant gaps between funded research and policy in Chile in themes like sustainable bioenergy production and copper mining and mineral processing techniques. These topics are prevalent in research. Our study corroborates previous research that stated that limited bioenergy development is due to limited policy support, public policy deficiencies, and absent regulation for waste management and energy recovery (Ludlow et al., 2021). Our study also supports the findings of Guzmán et al. (2023), who stated that there is a lack of mining policy in Chile and the calls of researchers for a comprehensive mineral policy that contributes to sustainable and economic development (González et al., 2023). The findings of our study are crucial for guiding future research and policy development in these areas.

Our study reported significant gaps between policy and funded research regarding themes related to the anatomy and physiology of aquatic and terrestrial organisms, taxonomy of insects, astronomy and observatories, oceanic islands, and marine biodiversity, which are prevalent in policy documents. Researchers stated that Chile has developed a thorough institutional policy framework for climate change mitigation and adaptation that includes measures for biodiversity adaptation in the aquaculture/marine sectors (Engler, 2024). Despite a solid policy framework on the matter, our study shows that more research is required on this subject. These findings are aligned with previous research that observed that the aquaculture and fisheries industries require a research agenda to respond to sustainable demands (Aguilera & Larraín, 2021). Researchers also call for a more critical assessment of the regulatory framework to promote the evolution and adaptation of the aquaculture sector (Engler, 2024) in order to preserve marine biodiversity. In terms of astronomy and observatories, despite the development of several policy reforms to foster installation of observatories, there is still need for further qualitative and quantitative research in the area (Guridi et al., 2020), which confirms our study's findings.

The gaps identified in our study serve as a call to action for policy makers and scholars to consider the need for fostering research and policy development. Research funding agencies can use our study's findings to guide project calls to areas that require further investigation. Our study identified prevalent topics in environmental policies that have not been sufficiently explored by researchers, suggesting that related policies may not have been adequately assessed and that improvements to these policies may not have been identified based on reliable scientific methods. However, it is equally important for policy makers to be alert to areas that have been investigated by scholars but lack corresponding policies. Moreover. the topics our study identified, such as gaps in policies related to copper mining and bioenergy production, are crucial for the country's environmental and economic development.

This study also addresses calls to support research whose findings will be regarded by decision-makers in policy, planning, and public administration (Newig & Rose, 2020) and will offer evidence-based suggestions to improve the regulatory structure and stakeholder engagement (Villacura et al., 2024). A more intense link between research and policy will support overcoming the challenges related to environmental issues (Vargas et al., 2022).

This study also extends previous research made in Chile, a country where the pressure on the use of natural resources has greatly increased in recent years (Carranza et al., 2020). Velasco and Mumme (2021) stated that resource-intensive development and socio-environmental tensions are the country's most pressing environmental problems. Our study extends research focused on Chile and Latin America by identifying gaps between research and policy that could be addressed by future studies.

Our study showed that the interest in environmental themes related to copper mining is prevalent in research. It is known that copper exports represent around a third of Chilean GDP and that mining has had an important role in tax revenue generation (Mardones & del Rio, 2019). It is known that most of Chile's copper reserves are in highly water-stressed regions (Fitzsimons & Warren, 2024), which corroborates the need to carry out research focused on developing more environment-friendly exploration techniques. Our study also extends previous bibliometric research that has identified research trends in biogas technology in rural development (Tundup et al., 2021). sections.paper_id: Barbosa and Gomes - 2026 - The interface between research funding and environmental policies in an emergent economy using neura
paragraphs: This study presents contributions to scholars, managers, research agencies, and policy makers. This study supports the decision-making process of funding agencies, especially in Chile and other emerging economies, by evaluating the similarities and gaps between research and public policies. Since public funding results in different types of impact (more scientific publications and better reputation), grants should be transparently and diversely allocated (Álvarez-Bornstein & Montesi, 2021). Although projects on sustainability might be more difficult to explain and justify due to the lack of a well-defined discipline (Bromham et al., 2016), funded research in the area of sustainability is likely to receive increasing resources and attention from public institutions, governments, and the society due to the pressures from these stakeholders and to challenges our planet and society face nowadays.

Funding bodies decide on the research that is supported, influencing the type of knowledge produced. In order to evaluate their decision and the quality of research being supported, these agencies must assess the impact of research funding in academic and societal contexts (Álvarez-Bornstein & Montesi, 2021). Thus, the use of public resources should be adequately justified through an exhaustive analysis of this policy (Mardones & Velásquez, 2021). Research should influence and improve sustainability-related policies. To do so, researchers and funders should have shared goals and a continuous dialogue throughout the research process (Campbell et al., 2015). By analyzing the alignment between public policies and research, this study calls attention to the fact that policy makers and funding agencies should further encourage and support projects that address sustainability problems and require external support (Sauermann et al., 2020). Especially in Chile and in countries with similar characteristics, public policies must be designed to stimulate scientific production and innovation (Krauskopf et al., 2007). Lastly, since policy learning is a long-term process (Tamtik, 2016), this study calls for rapid adjustments in environmental policy, so their outcomes will be seen sooner.

This study also contributes to the definition of environmental and sustainability public policies in Chile and in other economies with similar characteristics. Analyzing some of the country's environmental policies is relevant due to Chile's economic growth over the last few decades (López & Benavides, 2016). However, the country excessively depends on resource-intensive and raw material sectors, such as the mining industry. The average participation of the primary and extractive sectors in the country's GDP has been much higher than that of most middle-income countries. Hence, the country's economic growth has been associated with poor environmental performance (López & Benavides, 2016). Since these characteristics are shared among other emerging economies, especially in Latin America, we believe the findings of this study are extendable to other countries and should support policy making in other regions.

Finally, we understand this study calls the attention of other types of stakeholders (managers, customers, and nongovernmental organizations) to stimulate and pressure for innovations and changes in sustainability. The adoption of sustainability practices in a company or even in a supply chain is influenced by some stakeholders and pressures related to compliance with government rules and regulations. Companies that offer sustainable products or services develop a true competitive differentiator (Giunipero et al., 2022). Consequently, these stakeholders should pressure research institutions to develop studies focused on supporting the elaboration of public policies.

sections.paper_id: Barbosa and Gomes - 2026 - The interface between research funding and environmental policies in an emergent economy using neura
sections.header_raw: CONCLUSIONS AND LIMITATIONS TO THIS STUDY
sections.header_norm: conclusions and limitations to this study
paragraphs: This study identified and assessed the interface between research and environmental policies in Chile in the area of sustainability. To do so, the main research topics were identified and described with the support of a bibliometric analysis. Moreover, we used neural topic modeling to identify the main topics present in environmental public policies published by the Chilean Ministry of the Environment and the dataset of published articles. The outcomes of both analyses were semantically compared to identify links and gaps between the studies performed by the research community and the public policies of the area.

Our study identified the themes that have been both the subject of research and concern of environmental policies. These themes include waste management and recycling, GHG emissions, climate change, and forest management and conservation. Funded research and policy related to these topics are aligned in Chile. Our study also identified themes that have been prevalent in either research studies or policy documents. For these themes, our study emphasizes the need for further research to support policy development and assessment and for policy discussion on themes that have been deeply explored by funded research. Hence, this study's outcomes will guide future research project calls and future policy decisions related to environmental sustainability in the country.

Although this study presents important contributions to the area, it is not free of limitations. First, it is known that, despite the efforts of funding agencies to standardize the format for acknowledging research grants in published papers, authors can provide incomplete information about their funding grants which may lead to difficulties and errors in the identification of funders and grants (Álvarez-Bornstein et al., 2017). In addition, funding body acknowledgments in published papers occasionally contain multiple funding acknowledgments (Langfeldt et al., 2015;Rigby, 2013), so the part of the sample of studies analyzed could have been supported by international agencies as well. Second, we proposed a specific algorithm to detect gaps among the two groups of text (research and policy). We acknowledge that quantifying these gaps may be very challenging and different text analytics techniques could have been used. Finally, this study was focused on policies published by the Ministry of the Environment only. Public policies that establish action plans and guidelines related to the social dimension of sustainability were considered out of the scope of this study. So, research that explores this dimension is necessary and left as future work.
